content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 30,
1993
Joseph R.
Syinkowick
General Counsel
California Department of Education
P.O.
Box 944272
Sacramento,
CA
94244—2720
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93--115
Dear Mr.
Symkowick:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Sally Mentor,
Deputy Superintendent of Public Instruction for
Curriculum and Instructional Leadership,
under the “revolving
door”
and “ethics” provisions of the Political Reform Act.’!
QUESTIONS
1.
Does Section
87407 restrict Ms.
Mentor’s participation in
decisions affecting her prospective employer?
2.
What other provisions of the Political Reform Act would
affect Ms.
Mentor if she accepts
a position in the private sector?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Ms.
Mentor may not make, participate in making,
or use
her position to influence any governmental decision relating to
her prospective employer.
2.
Ms.
Mentor may not,
for one year following her
employment,
act as agent or attorney for,
or otherwise represent
any other person before the Department of Education,
for the
purpose of influencing any administrative action.
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section
18000
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95812
•
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No. A-93-l15
Page Two
In addition,
she may not, for compensation, act as agent or
attorney for,
or represent any other person before the Department
of Education, or any officer or employee thereof,
by making any
appearance or communication in connection with any proceeding in
which she participated.
This restriction has no time limit.
FACTS
Sally Mentor is the Deputy Superintendent of Public
Instruction for Curriculum and Instructional Leadership within the
Department of Education.
On March 7,
1993,
she was offered a
salaried position with the New Standards Project
(NSP).
The NSP is an outgrowth of the National Goals established by
the National Governor’s Association.
It is run by the University
of Pittsburg’s Learning Research and Development Center and the
National Center on Education and the Economy.
Twenty—two states,
as well as a number of large school systems,
have collaborated to
develop a national system of assessments tied to high standards
for student performance.’
The State of California is participating in NSP.
Ms.
Mentor
represented Superintendent Honig’s support for a membership fee
system for participating states and school systems.
The fee would
allow members the ability to get comparison scores.
California
has committed $175,000 for membership in NSP.
Ms.
Mentor,
on behalf of Superintendent Honig,
has
participated in various meetings between September of 1992 and
January 1993.
In her memorandum to you she indicated that she did
not participate in any decisions or votes.
In many instances,
she
attended as an observer.
According to NSP by—laws,
only
“principal” governing board members are allowed to participate and
vote.
In one instance,
NSP Co—Director Lauren Resnick consulted
with Bill Honig over the telephone prior to a governing board
meeting regarding the membership fee idea.
Mr.
Honig supported
the idea and Ms.
Mentor overheard that conversation.
During the
board meeting,
she did relay Mr.
Honig’s support to the governing
board.
On March 7,
1993,
Ms.
Mentor was offered a salaried position
with the New Standards Project in Washington,
D.C.
In a March 23,
1993 memorandum to Fred Tempes and Dale Carlson,
Ms.
Mentor
confirms a March 8,
1993 conversation wherein she advised
Mr.
Tempes and Mr.
Carlson of her decision to remove herself from
further participation as a member of the New Standards Project.
ANALYSIS
Section 87407 provides that no administrative official,
elected state officer,
or designated employee of the Legislature
shall make, participate in making,
or use their official position


Advice File No.
A-93-115
Page Three
to influence,
any governmental decision directly relating to any
person with whom the official is negotiating,
or has any
arrangement concerning, prospective employment.
“Administrative official” is defined as every member,
officer,
employee or consultant of a state administrative agency
who as part of the official responsibilities engages in any
judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a
purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity.
(Section
87400(b).)
Ms.
Mentor is the Deputy Superintendent of Public
Instruction.
As such,
she is an “administrative official” subject
to the prohibition in Section 87407.
She may not make,
participate in making,
or use her official position to influence
any governmental decision affecting NSP.
2
/
An official makes a governmental decision when the official:
(1)
Votes on a matter;
(2)
Appoints a person;
(3)
obligates or commits the agency to any course of
action;
(4)
Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
his or her agency;
(5)
Determines not to act, within the meaning of
subparagraphs
(1)
through
(4)
above, unless such
determination is made because of the official’s
financial interest.
Regulation 18700(b)
An official “participates in the making of a governmental
decision” when,
acting within the authority of the official’s
position, the official:
(1)
Negotiates, without significant substantive review,
with a governmental entity or private person
regarding the decision;
or
(2)
Advises or makes recommendations to the decision—
maker, either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, by
2/
The Commission has not adopted regulations which define
making, participating in the making or using your official
position to influence a decision in the context of Section 87407.
Accordingly,
we will apply the existing regulation used for the
purpose of determining conflicts of interests for our analysis.


Advice File No.
A—93—115
Page Four
(A)
Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise
of judgment on the part of the official
and the purpose of which is to influence
the decision;
or
(B)
Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally,
or in
writing,
which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and
the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.
Regulation 18700(c)
An official is “attempting to use his or her official
position” to influence a decision if,
for the purpose of
influencing the decision of the agency,
the official contacts,
or
appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member,
officer,
employee or consultant of the agency.
Attempts to
influence,
include,
but are not limited to,
appearances or
contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client or
customer.
(Regulation 18700.1(a).)
Accordingly,
Ms.
Mentor may not make or participate in the
making of decisions,
or use her official position to influence any
decision of the Department of Education,
as defined above,
directly relating to NSP, her prospective employer.
Ms.
Mentor indicated in a telephone conversation that she was
offered the position with NSP on Sunday,
March 7,
1993.
You
provided a copy of a March 23,
1993 Memorandum from Sally Mentor
to Fred Tempes and Dale Carlson confirming a conversation held on
March 8,
1993 regarding the decision to remove herself from any
future decision affecting the New Standards Project and advising
that future questions should be directed to the Acting State
Superintendent.
It appears that effective March 8,
1993,
Ms.
Mentor removed
herself from making or participating in any decisions affecting
the New Standards Project.
She should continue to abstain from
participating in such decisions throughout the remainder of her
employment with the Department of Education.
In addition to the prohibitions contained in Section 87407,
certain “revolving door” provisions will apply to Ms.
Mentor as
well.
Section 87406,
the
“Marks Postgovernment Employment
Restrictions Act,” provides that no member of a state
administrative agency,
for a period of one year after leaving
employment, shall,
for compensation, act as agent or attorney for,


Advice File No.
A-93-115
Page Five
or otherwise represent,
any other person,
by making any formal or
informal appearance,
or by making any oral or written
communication,
before any state administrative agency,
or officer
or employee therefore,
for which the official worked or
representative during the
12 months before leaving employment.
This section prohibits the appearance or communication by the
official for the purpose of influencing administrative action,
or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance,
amendment,
awarding,
or revocation of a permit,
license, grant,
or
contract,
or the sale or purchase of goods or property.
Ms.
Mentor would not be permitted to represent NSP before the
Department of Education for a one-year period.
Section 87401,
a similar revolving door provision,
is more
restrictive.
It provides that no former state administrative
official, after the termination of employment, shall for
compensation act as agent or attorney for,
or otherwise represent,
any other person before any court or state administrative agency
or any officer or employee thereof by making any formal or
informal appearance,
or by making any oral or written
communication with the intent to influence,
in connection with any
judicial,
quasi—judicial or other proceeding if the State of
California is a part or has a direct and substantial interest and
the proceeding is one in which the former state administrative
official participated.
Unlike Section 87406, this restriction is
for an unlimited duration.
Accordingly,
the Political Reform Act requires that
Ms. Mentor abstain from making or participating in the making,
or
using her official position with the Department of Education to
influence any decision affecting NSP,
her prospective employer.
In addition,
once Ms.
Mentor is employed with NSP,
the Act’s
revolving door provisions may prevent her from participating in
certain proceedings before the Department of Education.
I hope this has been helpful to you.
If you have any other
questions,
or need to discuss this further, please feel free to
contact the Legal Division at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
Jeanette E.
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
IM/JET/jt

a position in the private sector?

Does Section
87407 restrict Ms.
Mentor’s participation in
decisions affecting her prospective employer?
2.
What other provisions of the Political Reform Act would
affect Ms.
Mentor if she accepts
a position in the private sector?

CONCLUSION: 1.
Ms.
Mentor may not make, participate in making,
or use
her position to influence any governmental decision relating to
her prospective employer.
2.
Ms.
Mentor may not,
for one year following her
employment,
act as agent or attorney for,
or otherwise represent
any other person before the Department of Education,
for the

2,
